subject: ap-psychology
theme: Testing and Individual Differences: Measuring the Mind

core_concepts.title: Principles of Psychological Testing
core_concepts.summary: Reliability is the consistency of a test — test-retest reliability (same results across time), inter-rater reliability (same results across scorers), and split-half reliability (two halves correlate). Validity is whether a test measures what it claims — content validity (covers the full domain), predictive validity (predicts future outcomes), and construct validity (measures the intended construct). A test can be reliable but not valid (consistent but consistently wrong, like a miscalibrated scale). Standardization involves uniform procedures and norms — comparing individual scores to a representative reference group. IQ scores follow a normal distribution with a mean of 100 and standard deviation of 15.

core_concepts.title: History of Intelligence Testing
core_concepts.summary: Alfred Binet and Theodore Simon created the first practical intelligence test (1905) to identify French schoolchildren needing educational support — Binet believed intelligence was not fixed. Lewis Terman adapted Binet's test into the Stanford-Binet and introduced the IQ formula: IQ = (mental age / chronological age) × 100. David Wechsler developed the WAIS (Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale) and WISC (for children), providing separate verbal and nonverbal (performance) scores — now the most widely used intelligence tests. Modern tests use deviation IQ (standard scores) rather than the original ratio formula.

core_concepts.title: Theories of Intelligence
core_concepts.summary: Charles Spearman proposed that factor analysis reveals a general intelligence factor 'g' underlying all cognitive performance plus specific 's' factors. Gardner's multiple intelligences theory proposes 8+ independent intelligences (linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist) that IQ tests underrepresent. Sternberg's triarchic theory proposes three types: analytical (academic), creative (novel problem-solving), and practical ('street smarts'). Cattell distinguished fluid intelligence (novel problem-solving, peaks early, declines with age) from crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge and vocabulary, stable or increases into old age). Savant syndrome supports Gardner's view of multiple independent intelligences.

core_concepts.title: Nature, Nurture, and Group Differences
core_concepts.summary: Twin and adoption studies show significant genetic contributions to intelligence — identical twins reared apart have higher IQ correlations (~.75) than fraternal twins reared together (~.60). Heritability (~50-80% in adults) is a population statistic about variance — it does NOT mean intelligence is fixed or unresponsive to environment. The Flynn effect documents rising IQ scores across generations, demonstrating environmental influences. Group differences in average IQ scores are primarily explained by environmental factors (socioeconomic inequality, educational access, stereotype threat, test bias) — not genetic group differences. Heritability increasing with age reflects gene-environment correlation through self-selection of environments.

core_concepts.title: Stereotype Threat and Test Bias
core_concepts.summary: Claude Steele's stereotype threat research shows that members of groups targeted by negative stereotypes perform worse on tests when the stereotype is made salient — the anxiety of potentially confirming the stereotype consumes cognitive resources and impairs performance. This was demonstrated by Steele and Aronson (1995) showing Black students underperformed White students only when the test was framed as a diagnostic intelligence measure. Cultural test bias occurs when test content reflects cultural knowledge of the dominant group rather than the intended construct — tests developed in Western cultures may systematically disadvantage people from other cultural backgrounds. Removing stereotype threat cues (e.g., framing test as non-diagnostic) reduces performance gaps.

key_terms.definition: Consistency of a test — producing the same results across administrations, raters, or test halves
Consistency of scores when the same test is given twice to the same people
Consistency of scoring between different raters or judges
Whether a test actually measures what it claims to measure
Whether test items adequately represent the full domain being assessed
How well test scores predict future performance or behavior
Whether a test measures the theoretical construct it claims to measure
Uniform procedures and norms that allow comparison of scores across individuals
Bell-shaped curve — IQ scores are designed to follow a normal distribution with mean 100, SD 15
Original formula: (mental age / chronological age) × 100; modern tests use deviation scores
Terman's revision of Binet's test; introduced the IQ formula
Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale — provides verbal and nonverbal ability scores separately
Spearman's factor underlying performance across all cognitive tests
Gardner's theory that intelligence includes 8+ distinct abilities beyond what IQ tests measure
Sternberg's theory of analytical, creative, and practical intelligence
Ability to reason flexibly and solve novel problems — peaks in young adulthood, declines with age
Accumulated knowledge and verbal skills built from experience — stable or increases with age
Population statistic indicating what proportion of variance in a trait is due to genetic differences
Generational rise in average IQ scores over the 20th century, suggesting environmental influences
Situational anxiety about confirming a negative stereotype that impairs test performance (Steele)
When test content favors the cultural background of one group over others, affecting validity
Test measuring what has been learned in a specific area
Test designed to predict future performance potential
Diagnosis requiring IQ below ~70 AND significant deficits in adaptive functioning

exam_tip: Reliability vs. validity is a core distinction — remember a test can be reliable without being valid (a miscalibrated scale is consistently wrong). On the AP exam, heritability is often tested: heritability is a population statistic about variance, NOT a statement about how much of an individual's IQ is 'from genes,' and high heritability does NOT mean intelligence cannot be changed by environment (as proven by the Flynn effect). Stereotype threat is a frequent stimulus question topic — know Steele and Aronson's experimental design and why the 'intelligence test' framing impairs performance.
